questions.question_text:
Find the cost of 
(i) ₹ 7200, 8% stock at 90 
(ii) ₹ 4500, 8.5% stock at 4 premium
(iii) ₹ 6400, 10% stock at 15 discount
questions.solution:
(i) Cost of ₹ 100 stock = ₹ 90.
Cost of ₹ 7200 stock = ₹ $\left(\frac{90}{100}\times7200\right)$ = ₹ 6480.
(ii) Cost of ₹ 100 stock = ₹ $(100 + 4)$ = ₹ 104.
Cost of ₹ 4500 stock = ₹ $\left(\frac{104}{100}\times4500\right)$ = ₹ 4680.
(iii) Cost of ₹ 100 stock = ₹ $(100 - 15)$ = ₹ 85.
Cost of ₹ 6400 stock = ₹ $\left(\frac{85}{100}\times6400\right)$ = ₹ 5440.

questions.question_text: Find the cash required to purchase ₹ 3200, $7\frac{1}{2}\%$ stock at 107 (brokerage $\frac{1}{2}\%$).
questions.solution:
Cash required to purchase ₹ 100 stock = ₹ $\left(107 + \frac{1}{2}\right)$ = ₹ $\frac{215}{2}$.
Cash required to purchase ₹ 3200 stock = ₹ $\left(\frac{215}{2} \times \frac{1}{100} \times 3200\right)$ = ₹ 3440.

questions.question_text: Find the cash realised by selling ₹ 2440, 9.5% stock at 4 discount (brokerage $\frac{1}{4}\%$).
questions.solution:
By selling ₹ 100 stock, cash realised = ₹ $\left[ (100 - 4) - \frac{1}{4} \right]$ = ₹ $\frac{383}{4}$.
By selling ₹ 2400 stock, cash realised = ₹ $\left( \frac{383}{4} \times \frac{1}{100} \times 2400 \right)$ = ₹ 2298.

questions.question_text: Find the annual income derived from ₹ 2500, 8% stock at 106.
questions.solution:
Income from ₹ 100 stock = ₹ 8.
Income from ₹ 2500 stock = ₹ $\left( \frac{8}{100} \times 2500 \right)$ = ₹ 200.

questions.question_text: Find the annual income derived by investing ₹ 6800 in 10% stock at 136.
questions.solution:
By investing ₹ 136, income obtained = ₹ 10.
By investing ₹ 6800, income obtained = ₹ $\left( \frac{10}{136} \times 6800 \right)$ = ₹ 500.

questions.question_text: Which is better investment? $7\frac{1}{2}\%$ stock at 105 or $6\frac{1}{2}\%$ stock at 94.
questions.solution:
Let the investment in each case be ₹ $(105 \times 94)$.
Case I : $7\frac{1}{2}\%$ stock at 105 :
On investing ₹ 105, income = ₹ $\frac{15}{2}$.
On investing ₹ $(105 \times 94)$, income = ₹ $\left( \frac{15}{2} \times \frac{1}{105} \times 105 \times 94 \right)$ = ₹ 705.
Case II : $6\frac{1}{2}\%$ stock at 94 :
On investing ₹ 94, income = ₹ $\frac{13}{2}$.
On investing ₹ $(105 \times 94)$, income = ₹ $\left( \frac{13}{2} \times \frac{1}{94} \times 105 \times 94 \right)$ = ₹ 682.50.
Clearly, the income from $7\frac{1}{2}\%$ stock at 105 is more.

questions.question_text: Find the cost of 96 shares of ₹ 10 each at $\frac{3}{4}$ discount, brokerage being $\frac{1}{4}$ per share.
questions.solution:
Cost of 1 share = ₹ $\left[ \left( 10 - \frac{3}{4} \right) + \frac{1}{4} \right]$ = ₹ $\frac{19}{2}$.
Cost of 96 shares = ₹ $\left( \frac{19}{2} \times 96 \right)$ = ₹ 912.

questions.question_text: Chinmay invested 25%, 30% and 20% of his savings in buying shares of three different companies P, Q and R which declared dividends 10%, 12% and 15% respectively. If his total income on account of dividends be ₹ 5460, find the amount he invested in buying shares of the company Q.
questions.solution:
Let Chinmay’s savings be ₹ x.
Then, amount invested in shares of company P = 25% of ₹ x;
amount invested in shares of company Q = 30% of ₹ x;
amount invested in shares of company R = 20% of ₹ x.
Total income = ₹ (10% of 25% of x + 12% of 30% of x + 15% of 20% of x)
= ₹ $\left( \frac{250}{10000}x + \frac{360}{10000}x + \frac{300}{10000}x \right)$ = ₹ $\frac{910}{10000}x$.
$\therefore \frac{910}{10000}x = 5460 \Rightarrow x = \frac{5460 \times 10000}{910} = 60000$.
Hence, amount invested in shares of company Q = 30% of ₹ 60000 = ₹ 18000.

questions.question_text: Find the income derived from 88 shares of ₹ 25 each at 5 premium, brokerage being $\frac{1}{4}$ per share and the rate of dividend being $7\frac{1}{2}\%$ per annum. Also, find the rate of interest on the investment.
questions.solution:
Cost of 1 share = ₹ $\left( 25 + 5 + \frac{1}{4} \right)$ = ₹ $\frac{121}{4}$.
Cost of 88 shares = ₹ $\left( \frac{121}{4} \times 88 \right)$ = ₹ 2662.
$\therefore$ Investment made = ₹ 2662.
Face value of 88 shares = ₹ $(88 \times 25)$ = ₹ 2200.
Dividend on ₹ 100 = ₹ $\frac{15}{2}$. Dividend on ₹ 2200 = ₹ $\left( \frac{15}{2} \times \frac{1}{100} \times 2200 \right)$ = ₹ 165.
$\therefore$ Income derived = ₹ 165.
Rate of interest on investment = $\left( \frac{165}{2662} \times 100 \right)$ = 6.2%.

questions.question_text: Ravi invested ₹ 913 partly in 4% stock at ₹ 97 and partly in 5% stock at ₹ 107. If his income form both is equal, find the amount invested in each stock.
questions.solution:
Let the investment in 4% stock be ₹ x.
Then, investment in 5% stock = ₹ $(913 - x)$.
$\frac{4}{97}x = \frac{5}{107}(913 - x) \Leftrightarrow \frac{4}{97}x + \frac{5}{107}x = \frac{4565}{107}$
$\Leftrightarrow \frac{428x + 485x}{97 \times 107} = \frac{4565}{107} \Leftrightarrow 913x = \frac{4565 \times 97 \times 107}{107}$
$\Leftrightarrow x = \frac{4565 \times 97}{913} = 485$.
Hence, amount invested in 4% stock = ₹ 485.
And, amount invested in 5% stock = ₹ $(913 - 485) = 428$.

questions.question_text: A man buys ₹ 25 shares in a company which pays 9% dividend. The money invested is such that it gives 10% on investment. At what price did he buy the shares?
questions.solution:
Suppose he buys each share for ₹ x.
Then, $\left( 25 \times \frac{9}{100} \right) = \left( x \times \frac{10}{100} \right)$ or x = 22.50.
$\therefore$ Cost of each share = ₹ 22.50.

questions.question_text: A man sells ₹ 5000, 12% stock at 156 and invests the proceeds partly in 8% stock at 90 and 9% stock at 108. He thereby increases his income by ₹ 70. How much of the proceeds were invested in each stock?
questions.solution:
S.P. of ₹ 5000 stock = ₹ $\left( \frac{156}{100} \times 5000 \right)$ = ₹ 7800.
Income from this stock = ₹ $\left( \frac{12}{100} \times 5000 \right)$ = ₹ 600.
Let investment in 8% stock be ₹ x and that in 9% stock = ₹ $(7800 - x)$.
$\therefore \left( x \times \frac{8}{90} \right) + \left( (7800 - x) \times \frac{9}{108} \right) = (600 + 70)$
$\Leftrightarrow \frac{4x}{45} + \frac{7800 - x}{12} = 670 \Leftrightarrow 16x + 117000 - 15x = (670 \times 180) \Leftrightarrow x = 3600$.
$\therefore$ Money invested in 8% stock at 90 = ₹ 3600.
Money invested in 9% at 108 = ₹ $(7800 - 3600) = 4200$.

questions.question_text: The cost price of a ₹ 100 stock at 4 discount, when brokerage is $\frac{1}{4}\%$ is
The cash realised on selling a 14% stock at ₹ 106.25, brokerage being $\frac{1}{4}\%$, is
How many shares of market value ₹ 25 each can be purchased for ₹ 12750, brokerage being 2%?
questions.solution:
C.P. of each share = ₹ (25 + 2% of 25) = ₹ 25.50.
\ Number of shares = $\left(\frac{12750}{25.50}\right)$ = 500.

questions.question_text: A man invests in a 16% stock at 128. The interest obtained by him is
questions.solution:
By investing ₹ 128, income derived = ₹ 16.
By investing ₹ 100, income derived = ₹ $\left(\frac{16}{128}\times100\right)$ = 12.5.
\ Interest obtained = 12.5%.

questions.question_text: The income derived from a ₹ 100, 13% stock at ₹ 105, is
questions.solution: Income on ₹ 100 stock = ₹ 13.

questions.question_text: A wants to secure an annual income of ₹ 1500 by investing in 15% debentures of face value ₹ 100 each and available for ₹ 104 each. If the brokerage is 1%, then the sum of money he should invest is
questions.solution:
Income on each debenture = 15% of ₹ 100 = ₹ 15.
Number of debentures required = ₹ $\left(\frac{1500}{15}\right)$ = 100.
Cost of each debenture
= ₹ (104 + 1% of 104) = (104 + 1.04) = ₹ 105.04.
\ Total investment = ₹ (105.04 $\times$ 100) = ₹ 10504.

questions.question_text: A man invested ₹ 4455 in ₹ 10 shares quoted at ₹ 8.25. If the rate of dividend be 12%, his annual income is :
questions.solution:
Number of shares = $\left(\frac{4455}{8.25}\right)$ = 540.
Face value = ₹ (540 $\times$ 10) = ₹ 5400.
Annual income = ₹ $\left(\frac{12}{100}\times5400\right)$ = ₹ 648.

questions.question_text: A man invested ₹ 14,400 in ₹ 100 shares of a company at 20% premium. If the company declares 5% dividend at the end of the year, then how much does he get?
questions.solution:
Number of shares = $\left(\frac{14400}{120}\right)$ = 120.
Face value = ₹ (100 $\times$ 120) = ₹ 12000.
Annual income = ₹ $\left(\frac{5}{100}\times12000\right)$ = ₹ 600.

questions.question_text: A person has deposited ₹ 13200 in a bank which pays 14% interest. He withdraws the money and invests in ₹ 100 stock at ₹ 110 which pays a dividend of 15%. How much does he gain or lose?
questions.solution:
Income from bank = 14% of ₹ 13200 = ₹ 1848.
Number of shares purchased = ₹ $\left(\frac{13200}{110}\right)$ = 120.
Income from stock
= (15% of ₹ 100) $\times$ 120 = ₹ (15 $\times$ 120) = ₹ 1800.
\ Loss = ₹ (1848 – 1800) = ₹ 48.

questions.question_text: A 6% stock yields 8%. The market value of the stock is
questions.solution:
For an income of ₹ 8, investment = ₹ 100.
For an income of ₹ 6, investment = ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{8}\times6\right)$ = ₹ 75.
\ Market value of ₹ 100 stock = ₹ 75.

questions.question_text: A 9% stock yields 8%. The market value of the stock is
questions.solution:
To obtain ₹ 8, investment = ₹ 100.
To obtain ₹ 9, investment = ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{8}\times9\right)$ = ₹ 112.50.
\ Market value of ₹ 100 stock = ₹ 112.50.

questions.question_text: A 12% stock yielding 10% is quoted at
questions.solution:
To earn ₹ 10, money invested = ₹ 100.
To earn ₹ 12, money invested = ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{10}\times12\right)$ = ₹ 120.
\ Market value of ₹ 100 stock = ₹ 120.

questions.question_text: By investing ₹ 3450 in a $4\frac{1}{2}\%$ stock, a man obtains an income of ₹ 150. Find the market price of the stock.
questions.solution:
To earn ₹ 150, investment = ₹ 3450.
To earn ₹ 4.50, investment = ₹ $\left(\frac{3450}{150}\times4.50\right)$ = ₹ 103.50.
\ Market value of ₹ 100 stock = ₹ 103.50.

questions.question_text: To produce an annual income of ₹ 1200 from a 12% stock at 90, the amount of stock needed is
questions.solution:
For an income of ₹ 12, stock needed = ₹ 100.
For an income of ₹ 1200, stock needed
= ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{12}\times1200\right)$ = ₹ 10,000.

questions.question_text: In order to obtain an income of ₹ 650 from 10% stock at ₹ 96, one must make an investment of
questions.solution:
To obtain ₹ 10, investment = ₹ 96.
To obtain ₹ 650, investment = ₹ $\left(\frac{96}{10}\times650\right)$ = ₹ 6240.

questions.question_text: By investing in $16\frac{2}{3}\%$ stock at 64, one earns ₹ 1500. The investment made is
questions.solution:
To earn ₹ $\frac{50}{3}$, investment = ₹ 64.
To earn ₹ 1500, investment = ₹ $\left(\frac{64}{50/3}\times1500\right)$ = ₹ 5760.

questions.question_text: A man invested ₹ 1552 in a stock at 97 to obtain an income of ₹ 128. The dividend from the stock is
questions.solution:
By investing ₹ 1552, income = ₹ 128.
By investing ₹ 97, income = ₹ $\left(\frac{128}{1552}\times97\right)$ = ₹ 8.
\ Dividend = 8%.

questions.question_text: A man bought 20 shares of ₹ 50 at 5 discount, the rate of dividend being $13\frac{1}{2}\%$. The rate of interest obtained is :
questions.solution:
Investment = ₹ [20 $\times$ (50 – 5)] = ₹ 900.
Face value = ₹ (50 $\times$ 20) = ₹ 1000.
Dividend = ₹ $\left(\frac{27}{2}\times\frac{1000}{100}\right)$ = ₹ 135.
Interest obtained = $\left(\frac{135}{900}\times100\right)$% = 15%.

questions.question_text: At what price should I buy a share the value of which is ₹ 100, paying a dividend of 8% so that my yield is 11%?
questions.solution:
Dividend on ₹ 100 = 8% of ₹ 100 = ₹ 8.
₹ 11 is an income on ₹ 100.
\ ₹ 8 is an income on ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{11}\times8\right)$ = ₹ 72.72.

questions.question_text: A man buys ₹ 50 shares in a company which pays 10% dividend. If the man gets 12.5% on his investment, at what price did he buy the shares?
questions.solution:
Dividend on 1 share = $\left(\frac{10}{100}\times50\right)$ = ₹ 5.
₹ 12.50 is an income on an investment of ₹ 100.
₹ 5 is an income on an investment of ₹ $\left(\frac{100}{12.5}\times5\right)$ = ₹ 40.
\ Cost of 1 share = ₹ 40.

questions.question_text: The market value of a 10.5% stock, in which an income of ₹ 756 is derived by investing ₹ 9000, brokerage being $\frac{1}{4}\%$, is
questions.solution:
For an income of ₹ 756, investment = ₹ 9000.
For an income of ₹ $ rac{21}{2}$, investment = ₹ $ rac{9000	imes21}{756	imes2}$ = ₹ 125.
\ For a ₹ 100 stock, investment = ₹ 125.
Market value of ₹ 100 stock = ₹ $ rac{1}{4}$ (125 - $ rac{1}{4}$) = ₹ 124.75.

questions.question_text: Sakshi invests a part of ₹ 12,000 in 12% stock at ₹ 120 and the remainder in 15% stock at ₹ 125. If his total dividend per annum is ₹ 1360, how much does he invest in 12% stock at ₹ 120?
₹ 9800 are invested partly in 9% stock at 75 and 10% stock at 80 to have equal amount of incomes. The investment in 9% stock is
questions.solution:
Let the investment in 9% stock be ₹ $x$.
Then, investment in 10% stock = ₹ (9800 - $x$).
$ rac{9}{75}x =  rac{10}{80}(9800 - x)$
$ rac{3}{25}x =  rac{1}{8}(9800 - x)$
$24x = 9800 	imes 25 - 25x$
$49x = 9800 	imes 25$
$x = 5000$.

questions.question_text: A person wants to invest ₹ 140000 in two types of bonds. The annual return is 12% on bond A and 16% on bond B. One of the conditions requires that the investment in bond B cannot be more than 40% of the investment in bond A. What is the maximum return he can get per year?
questions.solution:
Let the investment in Bond A be ₹ $x$. Then, investment in Bond B = ₹ (140000 - $x$).
For maximum return, we have :
140000 - $x$ = 40% of $x$ $\Rightarrow$ 140000 - $x$ = $ rac{2}{5}x$
$\Rightarrow$ $ rac{7}{5}x$ = 140000 $\Rightarrow$ $x$ = $ rac{140000 	imes 5}{7}$ = 100000.
So, investment in Bond A = ₹ 100000;
Investment in Bond B = ₹ (140000 - 100000) = ₹ 40000.
\ Maximum return = 12% of ₹ 100000 + 16% of ₹ 40000
= ₹ (12000 + 4800) = ₹ 16800.

questions.question_text: A man invests some money partly in 9% stock at 96 and partly in 12% stock at 120. To obtain equal dividends from both, he must invest the money in the ratio
questions.solution:
For an income of ₹ 1 in 9% stock at 96,
Investment = ₹ $ rac{96}{9}$ = ₹ $ rac{32}{3}$.
For an income of ₹ 1 in 12% stock at 120, investment
= ₹ $ rac{120}{12}$ = ₹ 10.
\ Ratio of investments = $ rac{32}{3}$ : 10 = 32 : 30 = 16 : 15.

questions.question_text: Which is better investment — 11% stock at 143 or $9\frac{3}{4}\%$ stock at 117?
questions.option_d: Cannot be compared, as the total mount of investment is not given
questions.solution:
Let investment in each case be ₹ (143 $	imes$ 117).
Income in 1st case = ₹ $ rac{11}{143} 	imes 143 	imes 117$ = ₹ 1287.
Income in 2nd case = ₹ $ rac{39}{4 	imes 117} 	imes 143 	imes 117$ = ₹ 1394.25.
Clearly, $14 rac{3}{4}$% stock at 117 is better.

questions.question_text: A person invests ₹ 5508 in 4% stock at 102. He afterwards sells out at 105 and reinvests in 5% stock at 126. What is the change in his income?
questions.solution:
Number of shares purchased = $ rac{5508}{102}$ = 54.
Income from each share = 4% of ₹ 100 = ₹ 4.
\ Original income = ₹ (54 $	imes$ 4) = ₹ 216.
Money incurred from sale of share
= ₹ (105 $	imes$ 54) = ₹ 5670.
Number of new shares purchased = $ rac{5670}{126}$ = 45.
New income = ₹ (45 $	imes$ 5) = ₹ 225.
\ Change in income = ₹ (225 - 216) = ₹ 9.

questions.question_text: A retired man sells out ₹ 7500 of a 10% stock at ₹ 105.50 and invests the proceeds in 14% stock at ₹ 124.50. What is the change in income if he pays a service charge of 0.5% of the face value on each transaction?
questions.solution:
Number of shares sold = $ rac{7500}{100}$ = 75.
Proceeds from sale of ₹ 7500 stock
= ₹ [(105.50 - 0.5) $	imes$ 75] = ₹ 7875.
Number of new shares purchased
= $ rac{7875}{124.50 + 0.50}$ = $ rac{7875}{125}$ = 63.
Original income = 10% of ₹ 7500 = ₹ 750.
New income = 14% of ₹ 6300 = ₹ $ rac{14 	imes 6300}{100}$ = ₹ 882.
\ Change in income = ₹ (882 - 750) = ₹ 132.

questions.question_text: Which is better investment, 12% stock at par with an income tax at the rate of 5 paise per rupee or
questions.solution:
Let investment in each case = ₹ (100 $	imes$ 120).
Income from 12% stock = ₹ $ rac{12}{100} 	imes 100 	imes 120$ = ₹ 1440.
Net income = ₹ (1440 - $ rac{5}{100} 	imes 1440$) = ₹ 1368.
Income from $14 rac{2}{7}$% stock = ₹ $ rac{100}{7 	imes 100} 	imes 100 	imes 120$ = ₹ 1428.57.
Clearly, $14 rac{2}{7}$% stock is better.

questions.question_text: A invested some money in 10% stock at 96. If B wants to invest in an equally good 12% stock, he must purchase a stock worth of :
questions.solution:
For an income of ₹ 10, investment = ₹ 96.
For an income of ₹ 12, investment
= ₹ $ rac{96}{10} 	imes 12$ = ₹ 115.20.
